walkthrough.title: Unfold the Score Track
walkthrough.text: Let's get Dixit set up! First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. This is the scoring track where everyone's rabbit pawn will hop along as they earn points. The track goes from zero to thirty, and the finish line is at thirty. Make sure everyone can see it.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Rabbit
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a cute wooden rabbit pawn in their favorite color. Place your rabbit on the zero space of the scoring track. These little bunnies will race around the board as you score points. Everyone loves picking their rabbit, so take a moment to admire them.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Voting Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each player a set of numbered voting tokens. These are small cardboard tiles numbered 1 through 6. You will use these to vote for which card you think belongs to the Storyteller each round. Keep them face down in front of you so nobody can see which number you are planning to vote for.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal the Image Cards
walkthrough.text: Now for the star of the show — the image cards. These are 84 beautifully surreal illustrated cards. Give the deck a thorough shuffle. Deal six cards face down to each player. These form your starting hand. Keep your cards secret from other players. Place the remaining deck face down near the board as the draw pile.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Storyteller
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the first Storyteller. You can do this randomly or just let the most imaginative person at the table volunteer. The Storyteller role will rotate clockwise after each round, so everyone gets their turn to spin a tale. Once you have your first Storyteller, you are ready to play!

summary.title: Unfold the Score Track
bullets: Place the game board centrally so everyone can see
Scoring track runs from 0 to 30

summary.title: Choose Your Rabbit
bullets: Each player picks a colored rabbit pawn
Place all rabbits on the 0 space of the score track

summary.title: Hand Out Voting Tokens
bullets: Each player receives numbered voting tokens (1–6)
Keep tokens face down in front of you

summary.title: Shuffle and Deal the Image Cards
bullets: Shuffle all 84 image cards thoroughly
Deal 6 cards face down to each player
Place remaining cards as a face-down draw pile

summary.title: Choose the First Storyteller
bullets: Pick a starting Storyteller randomly or by volunteer
Storyteller role rotates clockwise each round

walkthrough.title: The Storyteller Gives a Clue
walkthrough.text: Each round, one player is the Storyteller. The Storyteller looks at their six cards and picks one. Without showing it to anyone, they announce a clue for that card. The clue can be anything — a single word, a phrase, a sentence, a sound effect, a song lyric, even a gesture. The clue should relate to the image on the card in some way, but it does not have to be literal. Get creative!

walkthrough.title: Other Players Submit Cards
walkthrough.text: After hearing the clue, every other player looks at their own hand and picks the card that best matches the Storyteller's clue. You are trying to find a card from your hand that could plausibly be the one the Storyteller was describing. Hand your chosen card face down to the Storyteller. You cannot submit the Storyteller's actual card because it is not in your hand — you are always submitting a decoy from your own cards.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Reveal
walkthrough.text: The Storyteller collects all the submitted cards, adds their own card to the pile, and shuffles them together. Then they lay all the cards face up in a row on the table and number them left to right, starting with 1. Everyone can now see all the cards. This is the lineup — one of these cards is the Storyteller's, and the rest are decoys submitted by other players.

walkthrough.title: Vote for the Storyteller's Card
walkthrough.text: Now it is time to vote. Every player except the Storyteller picks one of their numbered voting tokens matching the position of the card they think is the Storyteller's original card. Place your voting token face down on the table. Important rule: you cannot vote for your own card. Once everyone has placed their token, flip them all face up at the same time to reveal the votes.

walkthrough.title: Scoring — The Sweet Spot
walkthrough.text: Here is where Dixit gets clever. If ALL players found the Storyteller's card, or if NOBODY found it, the Storyteller scores zero points and every other player scores 2 points. The Storyteller only scores if some but not all players found their card. When that happens, the Storyteller scores 3 points and each player who voted correctly also scores 3 points. The sweet spot for the Storyteller is a clue that is neither too obvious nor too obscure.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Points for Decoys
walkthrough.text: On top of the main scoring, any non-Storyteller player whose decoy card received votes from other players scores 1 bonus point per vote on their card. So if two people accidentally voted for your decoy thinking it was the Storyteller's card, you earn 2 bonus points. This rewards you for picking a card that really matched the clue. Decoy points can add up and are a real part of the strategy.

walkthrough.title: Draw Back Up and Rotate
walkthrough.text: After scoring, move your rabbit pawns along the track to reflect the new scores. Then discard all the cards that were played this round — they go to a discard pile and will not be reused. Every player draws one new card from the draw pile to bring their hand back up to six cards. The Storyteller role passes clockwise to the next player, and a new round begins.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the draw pile runs out and players cannot refill their hands. At that point, whoever has the most points on the score track wins! If there is a tie, the tied players share the victory. A typical game lasts about 30 minutes, and the shifting Storyteller role means everyone gets to flex their creative muscles.

bullets: Storyteller picks one card from hand (keeps it secret)

summary.title: Other Players Submit Cards
bullets: Each other player picks their best-matching card from hand

Lay all cards face up in a row, numbered left to right

You cannot vote for your own card

SOME find it: Storyteller gets 3, correct voters get 3
Sweet spot: clue should be neither too obvious nor too obscure

Rewards picking a card that convincingly matches the clue

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Dixit
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through three complete rounds with four example players — Alex, Bailey, Casey, and Dana. Each round will show you a different Storytelling scenario, including a couple of common mistakes. Follow along even if your group is a different size. Ready to get creative? Let's go!

walkthrough.title: The Table is Set
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their six image cards in hand, their voting tokens, and a rabbit on zero. Alex is the first Storyteller. Remember, the Storyteller's goal is to give a clue that some but not all players will figure out. Too easy and you score nothing. Too hard and you also score nothing. Aim for the sweet spot.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — Alex Gives a Clue
walkthrough.text: Alex looks at their six cards. One shows a surreal image of a giant fish swimming through clouds. Alex says the clue: 'Flying.' Nice and simple. The other three players — Bailey, Casey, and Dana — each search their hands for a card that could match the word 'Flying.' Bailey finds a card with a bird soaring over mountains. Casey picks a card with a kite caught in a tree. Dana submits a card showing a hot air balloon. All cards are handed face down to Alex.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — The Mistake: Too Obvious
walkthrough.text: Alex shuffles the four cards together, lays them out numbered 1 through 4, and everyone votes. The problem? Every single player votes for Alex's fish-in-the-clouds card. It was obviously the most literal match for 'Flying.' Because ALL players found the Storyteller's card, Alex scores 0 points. Bailey, Casey, and Dana each score 2 points. Ouch!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Be Too Literal
walkthrough.text: The takeaway from Round 1: if your clue directly describes what is on your card, everyone will spot it immediately. Instead of 'Flying,' Alex could have said something more abstract like 'Impossible journey' or 'Dreamscape.' A good clue connects to your card through a feeling, a memory, or an association — not a dictionary definition. The best clues make people think.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Bailey's Turn as Storyteller
walkthrough.text: Storyteller role rotates clockwise to Bailey. Bailey looks at their cards and picks one showing a child sitting inside a giant seashell. Bailey wants to avoid Alex's mistake of being too obvious, so they go in the opposite direction and announce a very abstract clue: 'Seventeen.' The other players are confused. Alex, Casey, and Dana stare at their cards trying to figure out how any of them relate to the number seventeen.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — The Mistake: Too Obscure
walkthrough.text: The cards are laid out and votes are cast. Nobody votes for Bailey's card. Not a single player. Casey's random submission gets two votes by accident, and Dana's gets one. Because NOBODY found the Storyteller's card, Bailey scores 0 points again! Alex, Casey, and Dana each score 2 points. Casey also earns 2 bonus points for the votes on their decoy, and Dana earns 1 bonus point.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Be Too Abstract
walkthrough.text: Bailey's clue was a personal reference that only they understood — maybe they went to the beach when they were seventeen. But nobody else could possibly make that connection. If zero players find your card, you score just as badly as if everyone finds it. Your clue needs to be something that at least one person at the table can connect to your image. Think about what the other players might see in your card, not just what you see.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 — Casey Nails It
walkthrough.text: Now it is Casey's turn as Storyteller. Casey picks a card showing a tree with doors and windows built into the trunk. Casey thinks for a moment and says: 'Home is where the heart is.' This is a great clue. It is not literally describing the image, but it captures the feeling of the card — a cozy, lived-in place. Alex, Bailey, and Dana each submit their best match.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 — Scoring the Sweet Spot
walkthrough.text: The four cards go face up. Alex and Dana both vote for Casey's tree-house card — they connected the clue to the image. Bailey votes for Dana's card instead, which showed a cat curled up on a pillow. Two out of three players found the Storyteller's card. Casey scores 3 points! Alex and Dana each score 3 points for voting correctly. Bailey scores 0 for guessing wrong, but Dana picks up 1 bonus point because Bailey voted for her decoy. That is the sweet spot in action.

walkthrough.title: The Decoy Mistake
walkthrough.text: Bailey played a card showing a math equation on a chalkboard. It had absolutely nothing to do with 'Home is where the heart is.' Nobody even considered voting for it. Meanwhile, Dana submitted a cat on a pillow — cozy, warm, homey — and snagged a bonus vote. The lesson: when you submit a decoy, try to match the Storyteller's clue as closely as possible. A convincing decoy earns you bonus points and confuses the other voters.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise. Dana becomes the next Storyteller, then it cycles back to Alex. Each round follows the same rhythm: Storyteller gives a clue, everyone submits a card, cards are revealed, votes are cast, and points are scored. After scoring, discard the played cards and draw new ones to refill your hand to six. The game flows quickly once you get the rhythm down.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have just played through three rounds of Dixit and seen all three possible scoring outcomes: too obvious, too obscure, and the sweet spot. You have also learned that picking strong decoy cards matters. Remember: the best Storyteller clues live in the gray area between obvious and impossible. Now you are ready to play a full game!

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for clue-giving strategy and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to scoring rules and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for clue-giving strategy
Reference the Appendix for scoring rules and game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: The Golden Rule of Clue-Giving
walkthrough.text: The single most important thing in Dixit is calibrating your clue difficulty. If everyone finds your card, you score zero. If nobody finds your card, you also score zero. You only score when some players get it and others do not. Think of it like a riddle — hard enough to be interesting, easy enough to be solvable. Before you announce your clue, ask yourself: will at least one person at this table connect this clue to my card, and will at least one person be fooled? If the answer to both is yes, you have a good clue.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Audience
walkthrough.text: The best Dixit clues are tailored to the people at your table. A movie reference works great if everyone at the table has seen the film, but it is useless if nobody has. Similarly, an inside joke between you and one other player can be a brilliant clue — they will likely find your card, while others may not. Pay attention to who is at the table and what they know. Are they book lovers? Music fans? Into sports? The more you understand your fellow players, the better your clues will land.

walkthrough.title: Types of Clues That Work Well
walkthrough.text: There are many kinds of clues you can give, and variety keeps the game fresh. Try emotions: 'Melancholy' or 'Excitement.' Try movie or book titles: 'The Wizard of Oz' or 'Alice in Wonderland.' Try song lyrics or sounds. Try abstract concepts: 'Freedom' or 'Betrayal.' Try personal memories: 'Summer camp' or 'First day of school.' The more unexpected your clue type, the harder it is for other players to find your card — but make sure at least one person can still make the connection.

walkthrough.title: Submitting Strong Decoys
walkthrough.text: When you are not the Storyteller, your decoy card matters more than you might think. A convincing decoy that matches the clue well can steal votes from other players, earning you 1 bonus point per vote. Look at all six of your cards and pick the one that best fits the Storyteller's clue — not a random throwaway. Sometimes your decoy will match better than the Storyteller's own card, and that is perfectly fine. Those stolen votes add up over the course of a game.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Storyteller
walkthrough.text: When you are voting, pay attention to the Storyteller as a person, not just the clue. How does this player usually give clues? Are they literal or abstract? Do they reference pop culture or personal feelings? If the Storyteller loves puns, look for the card that fits a pun interpretation. If they tend toward emotions, look for the card that evokes the strongest feeling. Over time, you will get better at reading each Storyteller's style, which gives you a voting edge.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Strategy as a Voter
walkthrough.text: When voting, remember that you get 3 points for finding the Storyteller's card. That is a big reward. But if you are unsure between two cards, consider which one a decoy-submitter might have chosen. If a card feels too perfect — almost too on-the-nose — it might actually be a clever decoy. The Storyteller knows that being too obvious scores zero, so their card is often the second-most-obvious choice. Trust your gut but think one level deeper.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Player Count
walkthrough.text: Dixit plays differently at three players versus six. With three players, there are only three cards in the lineup, so it is easier to identify the Storyteller's card. Clues need to be more subtle at low player counts. With five or six players, there are more decoys to hide among, so the Storyteller can be slightly more obvious. Adjust your clue difficulty based on how many players are at the table.

walkthrough.title: Have Fun With It
walkthrough.text: Dixit is a party game at heart. The best moments are not always about scoring — they are about the creative connections people make. Laugh at the weird interpretations, appreciate the clever clues, and do not stress about winning. The scoring system is designed to keep everyone close, so enjoy the ride. The more relaxed and playful the atmosphere, the more creative and interesting the clues become.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Layer Clue
walkthrough.text: The strongest Dixit clues work on two levels simultaneously. The surface level is a reasonable connection that multiple cards could match. The deeper level is a specific detail that only your card has. For example, if your card shows a whale reading a book underwater, saying 'Library' works on the surface — any card with books might match. But a player who notices the underwater setting might connect 'Library' to a deeper association. The key is that the deeper layer should be discoverable but not obvious.

walkthrough.title: Targeting Specific Players
walkthrough.text: A powerful advanced technique is to give a clue aimed at one specific player. If you share a memory or reference with just one person at the table, use it. They will likely find your card while others are left guessing among the decoys. This is especially effective with four or more players, where you only need one correct guesser to score your 3 points. Be careful not to overuse this with the same partner, or others will catch on and start watching who you keep targeting.

walkthrough.title: The Decoy Arms Race
walkthrough.text: As players get better at Dixit, decoy selection becomes just as important as clue-giving. Experienced players will submit cards that genuinely match the clue, creating a lineup where every card looks plausible. To counter this, look at the lineup as a whole before voting. Which card feels like a match someone chose from their hand, and which card feels like the one that inspired the clue? The Storyteller's card often has a more organic, less forced connection to the clue.

walkthrough.title: Score Awareness
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on the score track. If you are in the lead, you can afford to take risks with more obscure clues — scoring zero as Storyteller is fine if everyone else only gains 2 points. If you are behind, you need to score every round. As a voter trailing the leader, remember that voting correctly gives you 3 points while the Storyteller also gets 3. Sometimes it is strategically better to submit a weak decoy on purpose so the Storyteller scores, rather than accidentally helping them get zero by matching the clue too well.

walkthrough.title: Exploiting Card Knowledge
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, pay attention to which cards other players have played. If you remember that Bailey played a lot of ocean-themed cards earlier and now has fewer of them, you know Bailey is less likely to have a strong water-themed decoy. This information helps you as both Storyteller and voter. As Storyteller, give a clue that matches a theme where fewer decoys exist in other hands. As a voter, a card that perfectly matches the clue is less suspicious if you know that player tends to have that type of card.

walkthrough.title: The Anti-Pattern Clue
walkthrough.text: Once your group gets comfortable, everyone develops a clue-giving pattern. Maybe you always give emotional clues, or you always reference movies. Break your pattern intentionally. If you have been giving abstract clues all game, suddenly give a very literal one-word clue. Your opponents who have been reading your style will overcorrect and look for something deeper that is not there. Pattern-breaking keeps you unpredictable and harder to read.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Decoy Points
walkthrough.text: Decoy bonus points are often the difference between winning and losing in experienced groups. When you are not the Storyteller, study the clue carefully and submit the single best-matching card in your hand. If you attract two or even three votes, that is 2 to 3 bonus points on top of any voting points you earn. Over a full game, consistently strong decoy submissions can earn you 8 to 12 extra points — enough to swing the outcome. Never waste your decoy submission on a random card.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Room
walkthrough.text: Advanced Dixit is as much about social deduction as it is about creativity. Watch body language when the cards are revealed. Does someone lean forward when they see a particular card? Does the Storyteller glance at one card more than others? These micro-reactions can tip you off. Similarly, control your own reactions. As Storyteller, practice a neutral expression when your card is revealed alongside the decoys. A poker face is an underrated Dixit skill.

bullets: Surface level: multiple cards could match the clue
Deeper level: a specific detail only your card has
The deeper layer should be discoverable but not obvious

Don't overuse the same target or others will catch on

entries.definition: The active player who selects a card from their hand and announces a clue for it. The Storyteller role rotates clockwise after each round. The Storyteller does not vote — only the other players vote.

The Storyteller's clue can be virtually anything: a single word, a phrase, a full sentence, a sound effect, a song, a gesture, a movie quote, or even a number. The only requirement is that the clue must relate to the chosen card in some way. Creativity is encouraged.

The 84 beautifully illustrated cards that form the core of the game. Each card features surreal, dreamlike artwork open to multiple interpretations. Players draw from this deck and always maintain a hand of six cards.

Numbered cardboard tiles (1 through 6) given to each player. Used to vote for which card in the lineup you believe is the Storyteller's card. You cannot vote for your own submitted card. Votes are placed face down and revealed simultaneously.

Cards submitted by non-Storyteller players that are mixed in with the Storyteller's card. The goal is to pick a decoy from your hand that matches the clue well enough to attract votes from other players, earning you 1 bonus point per vote received.

If ALL or NONE of the voters find the Storyteller's card, the Storyteller scores 0 and all other players score 2. If SOME (but not all) voters find it, the Storyteller and each correct voter score 3 points. Additionally, non-Storyteller players earn 1 bonus point per vote received on their decoy card.

The numbered track on the game board running from 0 to 30. Players move their rabbit pawns along the track as they score points. The first player to reach 30 (or the player with the most points when the deck runs out) wins the game.
At the end of each round, all played cards are discarded and each player draws one new card from the draw pile to return their hand to six cards. If the draw pile is empty, the game ends.

With only 3 players, each non-Storyteller submits 2 cards instead of 1, giving the Storyteller 5 cards total to shuffle (including their own). Each voter still casts only 1 vote. This adds more decoys to the lineup, making it harder to identify the Storyteller's card and balancing the lower player count.
Cute wooden rabbit-shaped player pieces in different colors. Each player places their rabbit on the scoring track and advances it as they earn points throughout the game.
